cc10x covers harness AND loop. Most tools only cover the loop.

The elephant in the room

"Loop engineering is a psyop."

What the skeptics say
r/ClaudeCode: "I am really struggling to believe that loop engineering is the applied AI skill I should be developing right now. Seems fishy."

The Register: "Loop engineering, latest AI buzzword, still needs humans in the loop."

Hacker News: "Tokenmaxxing with extra steps. Diminishes the work product of engineers."

They're right to be skeptical.
Why they're wrong
The skepticism is about hype without proof. And that's fair — most "loop engineering" content is think-pieces, not working systems.

But the pattern is real. Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) proved structured reflection adds +11 points on HumanEval. Boris Cherny isn't selling a course — he's describing how his team actually works.

The proof isn't in the buzzword. It's in the system.

A buzzword without a working system is hype. A working system without the vocabulary is unlabeled. CC10x is both.

Part I — Harness Engineering

What is a harness?

The harness is everything around the LLM call —
the scaffolding that determines what the model sees,
what it's allowed to do, and how its output is verified.
👁
Context control
What the model sees. Which files. Which history. Which constraints. Curated, not dumped.
🚧
Permission surface
What the model can touch. WRITE agents vs READ-ONLY agents. Roles enforced structurally, not by asking nicely.
✅
Verification gates
Evidence before advancement. No phase exits without proof. "exit 0 or it didn't happen."
Harness Engineering — CC10x as living proof

The router owns the harness.
Agents never see each other.

cc10x-router sole entry point
→
component-builder WRITE · TDD first
→
code-reviewer READ-ONLY · 6-pass adversarial
+
failure-hunter READ-ONLY · silent failures
→
integration-verifier exit 0 required
→
phase_exit_gate fail-closed
→
memory-update task-enforced
Deterministic routing
Router matches intent to workflow by keyword signal priority. No model judgment. No ambiguity. ERROR always beats BUILD.
Tiered model policy
Planner + verifier → capable model. Builder → standard. Doc-sync → cheap. Harness determines cost, not vibes.
Fail-closed gates
No phase advances on prose alone. CONTRACT YAML required. Missing or malformed? Workflow stops. Not retries — stops.
Harness Engineering — expertise injection

Skills are constraints,
not suggestions.

The full landscape — Andrew Ng's framework

Three loops. Three timescales.
One discipline.

⚡
Minute-scale loop
Agentic coding. Write code → run tests → fix failures → repeat. The tightest loop. This is what CC10x automates — BUILD → REVIEW → VERIFY → REMFIX, bounded at 3 cycles.

CC10x lives here
📅
Daily-scale loop
Build-test-ship. Ship a feature → gather feedback → learn → plan tomorrow's work. The team loop. CI/CD pipelines, PR review cycles, retrospective learnings stored in patterns.md.

CC10x feeds this
🎯
Weekly-scale loop
Product-market fit. Ship product → measure usage → adjust roadmap → re-plan. The strategic loop. The compounding knowledge in docs/solutions/ informs this loop across weeks and teams.

CC10x compounds this

Andrew Ng: "Loops are now a key part of how we get AI agents to iterate at length to build software."
— The minute-scale loop is the foundation. Without it, the daily and weekly loops have no signal to consume.

They're not separate disciplines
The harness defines the channel.
The loop fills it with signal.
Together they produce quality.
Without the harness, loops have no gates — you get infinite drift and no convergence signal.

→ Harness defines what counts as done.
Without loops, the harness is a one-shot executor — no correction, no learning, no recovery.

→ Loops close the quality gap the harness exposes.

text:
rationale. It reviews the plan against the codebase, not against the planner's confidence.

Agreement-first — planner returns NEEDS_CLARIFICATION on ambiguity. Never treats its own defaults as approved.

Doubt-Driven Development — for high-stakes decisions, an in-flight adversarial reviewer challenges the design BEFORE the plan is finalized.
Workflow deep-dive — DEBUG

DEBUG: evidence before any fix

routerintent: ERROR
→
bug-investigatorLOG FIRST · 10-rung feedback loop
→
code-reviewerreview the fix
→
integration-verifierregression test passes
→
memorygotcha persisted
Skills loaded
Investigator: debugging, building, verification, agent-common
Reviewer: code-review, verification, codebase-hygiene, agent-common
Contextual: research (if stuck), architecture (if prevention is architectural)

Tools: LSP (Go-to-Definition, Find References, Type Definition) for root-cause tracing — not just grep.
The loop
Feedback loop gate (MANDATORY): build repro signal BEFORE any hypothesis. No loop → fail closed.

Hypothesis loop: generate 3-5 ranked hypotheses → test → if all fail: NEEDS_EXTERNAL_RESEARCH

Fix loop: RED (failing test) → GREEN (minimal fix) → blast radius scan → verify

3 hypothesis cap then: research or BLOCKED
Why it works
No-Loop-No-Hypothesis gate — you can't guess without a repro. Prevents pattern-matching death spirals.

Causal chain gate — must explain the full chain from trigger to symptom. "Somehow X leads to Y" is a gap, not an answer. Predictions catch symptom fixes.

Debug memory — every failed hypothesis stored as [DEBUG-N] in activeContext.md. The system remembers what didn't work.
Workflow deep-dive — REVIEW + ORIENT

REVIEW: advisory only, never mutates

routerintent: REVIEW
→
code-reviewer6-pass · confidence ≥80
→
memorypersist findings
Skills loaded
Reviewer: code-review, verification, codebase-hygiene, agent-common
Contextual: frontend (if UI), architecture (if structural)

6 passes: Security → Performance → Quality → Friction → Plan Validity → Spec Compliance

Quantitative scoring: HARD/SOFT signals, min(HARD) capped by avg(SOFT)-10. Confidence ≥80 to report.
The loop
No remediation loop — REVIEW is advisory. It never creates REM-FIX tasks or mutates code.

If verdict is CHANGES_REQUESTED: router offers "Start BUILD to fix (Recommended)" — a transition, not an automatic action.

Zero-finding gate: zero findings on non-trivial change → confidence capped at 70. Must produce positive assertions with file:line evidence. No rubber-stamps.
ORIENT (read-only)
Zero agents. Zero tasks. Zero artifacts.

The router answers inline — maps modules, traces call hierarchy via LSP, explains in the project's own vocabulary.

"Help me understand this code" never falls through to BUILD and spawns a write agent. ORIENT precedes DEFAULT.

Why: orientation is the most common request. Routing it to BUILD would mean a builder modifying code you just wanted explained.
CC10x — the full system in one view

9 agents · 17 skills · 4 workflows
One coherent harness.

Router — the harness owner
Sole entry point. Owns all orchestration state. Agents cannot see each other or self-route. Workflow artifact is the single source of truth. Gates are fail-closed.
Agent chain — execution
9 agents: planner, component-builder, bug-investigator, code-reviewer, failure-hunter, integration-verifier, doc-syncer, plan-gap-reviewer, researcher. WRITE agents produce artifacts. READ-ONLY agents produce structured verdicts. Router decides next action from verdict.
Skills — expertise injection
17 skills loaded contextually by router. Building, debugging, code review, architecture, frontend, codebase hygiene, exploration, verification — expertise on demand, not always-on context.
Memory — loop persistence
activeContext.md · patterns.md · progress.md
What failed becomes a gotcha. What passed becomes a pattern. Loops improve over time.
Principles you can take home today

The 5 laws of harness + loop engineering.

1.
One entry point, one owner. The harness owns orchestration state. Agents execute; they never route.
2.
Gates are evidence, not time. A phase ends when proof exists — not when N seconds pass or the model says it's done.
3.
Loops have a ceiling. Unbounded loops drift. Hard limit + human checkpoint at N is a design constraint, not a workaround.
4.
Expertise is injected, not assumed. The harness decides what skills are in scope. Models don't self-select their constraints.
5.
Memory closes the loop across sessions. What failed once is a gotcha forever. The system gets smarter; the model doesn't have to.
Build your own

You don't need CC10x.
You need the pattern.

# The minimum viable harness def harness(request): intent = route(request) # deterministic context = load_memory() # structured skills = inject_hints(intent) # contextual for phase in intent.chain: result = run_agent( phase, context, skills ) if not gate_passes(result): return remediate(result) # loop persist_memory(result) # closes loop return result
route() — keyword signal → deterministic workflow. No model judgment at this layer.
gate_passes() — structured contract validation. YAML, not prose. Schema, not vibes.
remediate() — creates a scoped fix task with evidence. Bounded. Re-enters the loop with new context.
persist_memory() — gotchas, patterns, decisions. The loop learns even if the model doesn't.
The takeaway
Stop chasing better models.
Build a better harness.
Engineer your loops.

They move up — from prompting to designing the loops.

3 hypothesis cap then: research or BLOCKED

No-Loop-No-Hypothesis gate — you can't guess without a repro. Prevents pattern-matching death spirals.

Review complete. Findings written to the authoritative output path.

## Review Summary

**PASS (5 checks):**
- **Check 1** — All 21 slides share the same CSS class vocabulary (card, card-grid, chain, chain-node, pill, etc.). No invented classes.
- **Check 2** — Workflow slides 10a–10d are structurally consistent (eyebrow → h2@36px → chain → 3 cards). Minor note: 10d's chain is sparse (3 nodes) vs 10a/10b (7 nodes).
- **Check 3** — `document.querySelectorAll(".slide")` correctly picks up 21 slides. `.slide-counter` is a separate class token and won't match. Note: static fallback "1 / 15" at line 591 is stale but overwritten by JS on load.
- **Check 4** — HTML intact: 355/355 div balance, 30/30 comment markers, all self-closing, clean EOF.
- **Check 5** — All 21 slides have `data-section`. Workflow slides 10a–10d all belong to "Workflows" section.

**NOTE (1):**
- **Check 6** — No overflow (flex-wrap handles it), but 10a/10b chains wrap to 2 rows at 1100px max-width, breaking visual rhythm vs 10c/10d which fit one row.

**FAIL (3):**
- **Check 7** — Agent color mismatches: (a) Slide 4 renders `failure-hunter` BLUE (role=read, no override) but agent color is RED — same agent correctly shown RED in slide 10a; (b) Slide 10b renders `planner` GREEN (role=write, no override) but agent color is CYAN, while adjacent plan-gap-reviewer IS overridden to purple.
- **Check 8** — Slides 2c, 9b, 10a–10d exceed ~150 body words. Workflow card text at `font-size: 12px` is unreadable from a large hall.
- **Bonus** — Undefined CSS classes: `accent-cyan` (line 1819, slide 10b h2) and `accent-blue` (line 2002, slide 10d h2) are NOT in the stylesheet. Defined accents are `.accent`(cyan)/green/orange/purple/red/yellow. The headline accent phrases on 10b and 10d silently lose their intended color and render as plain white. Fix: use `class="accent"` for 10b; add inline `style="color: var(--blue)"` or a new `.accent-blue` rule for 10d.
